coredata.dc:title: Coke-derived graphene quantum dots as fluorescence nanoquencher in DNA detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yew, Ying Teng
Loo, Adeline Huiling
Sofer, Zdeněk
Klímová, Kateřina
Pumera, Martin
coredata.dc:description: The recent emergence of coal and related fossil fuel products as an abundant and affordable source of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) highlights the potential of the carbon precursor in the development of simple and cost-effective DNA nanobiosensors. A coke-derived precursor – calcined petroleum coke – was utilized for the facile one-step synthesis of GQDs. Thereafter, DNA detection based on the coke-derived GQD platform exploited the differences in propensity towards spontaneous self-assembly with labelled single-stranded, and double-stranded DNA sequences. For this purpose, GQDs functioned as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) acceptors for DNA detection down to 0.004nM. The sensing assay displays sensitivity over 3 orders of magnitudes. These results pave the way towards the identification of alternative nanomaterials for the development of inexpensive nanobiosensors for highly efficient, sensitive and selective detection of DNA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 2D materials
Biosensor
Genosensor
Fluorescence nanoparticle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Employing GQD as a fluorescence nanoquencher (FRET acceptor) in DNA analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: M.P. acknowledges a Tier 2 grant (MOE2013-T2-1-056; ARC 35/13) from the Ministry of Education, Singapore. Z.S. and K.K. were supported by Czech Science Foundation (GACR No. 15-09001S) and by specific university research (MSMT No. 20-SVV/2016). This work was supported by the project Advanced Functional Nanorobots (reg. No. CZ.02.1.01/0.0/0.0/15_003/0000444 financed by the EFRR).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM and (B) HR-TEM images of GQDs. Inset: SAED pattern of GQDs. Scale bar: (A) 50nm and (B) 20nm.
(A) DLS particle size distribution and (B) zeta potential distribution profile of GQDs.
(A) Wide-scan XPS and (B) high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of GQDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) corresponding fluorescence quenching of FAM-Lprobe (40nM) with increasing volumes of GQD (0, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 and 120μL). Conditions: Tris-HCl buffer (20mM, pH 7.4), λ ex =490nm. Extent of quenching is calculated as the change in PL intensity of FAM-Lprobe after incubation with GQD.
Calibration curve of FAM-Lprobe (40nM) with varying concentrations of cDNA target (0.004, 0.04, 0.4, 4, 40 and 400nM), in the presence of GQD. Conditions: Tris–HCl buffer (20mM, pH 7.4), λ ex =490nm.

Fluorescence emission spectra of FAM-Lprobe (40nM) following cDNA target (4nM) hybridization, in the presence and absence of GQD. Conditions: Tris–HCl buffer (20mM, pH 7.4), λ ex =490nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in text near the reference citation, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic representation of the proposed DNA sensing strategy based on GQD as a nanoquencher.

head.ce:title: Coke-derived graphene quantum dots as fluorescence nanoquencher in DNA detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Chemistry and Biological Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University, 21 Nanyang Link, Singapore 637371, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Chemistry and Biological Chemistry, School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, University of Chemistry and Technology Prague, Technická 5, 166 28 Prague 6, Czech Republic

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Inorganic Chemistry, University of Chemistry and Technology Prague

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The recent emergence of coal and related fossil fuel products as an abundant and affordable source of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) highlights the potential of the carbon precursor in the development of simple and cost-effective DNA nanobiosensors. A coke-derived precursor – calcined petroleum coke – was utilized for the facile one-step synthesis of GQDs. Thereafter, DNA detection based on the coke-derived GQD platform exploited the differences in propensity towards spontaneous self-assembly with labelled single-stranded, and double-stranded DNA sequences. For this purpose, GQDs functioned as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) acceptors for DNA detection down to 0.004nM. The sensing assay displays sensitivity over 3 orders of magnitudes. These results pave the way towards the identification of alternative nanomaterials for the development of inexpensive nanobiosensors for highly efficient, sensitive and selective detection of DNA.

ce:section.ce:para: Biosensors have witnessed a ubiquitous presence and increasing demand in the innovative research and commercial sectors, due to their widespread applications in medical diagnosis [1–4], pharmaceutical drug development [5], food quality assurance [6], environmental monitoring [7,8], military applications [9] and forensic analysis [10,11]. In particular, fluorescence-based deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) biosensors have played a prominent role due to the intrinsic physicochemical stability of genosensors [12], as well as high sensitivity [13,14], high target recognition specificity [15], favourable imaging properties [16,17] and simplicity of operation [13,15] achievable with fluorescence detection systems.

Such homogeneous fluorescence-based sensors typically exist as either fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) assays or quenching (static quenching or FRET quenching) assays [18]. The analytical performance of FRET sensors is highly dependent on the choice of donor-acceptor pair in the detection assay [19]. In order to circumvent this limitation, nanomaterials can serve as the quencher molecule, since they possess the capacity to quench over a range of emission wavelengths [20]. Additionally, nanoquenchers are capable of enhancing the signal-to-noise ratio of FRET genosensors [21]. Successfully employed nanostructures for this purpose include carbon and gold nanoparticles [20,22,23], graphene [24], graphene oxide [25,26], carbon nanotubes [19,27], carbon quantum dots [18], transition metal dichalcogenides [28,29], metal–organic frameworks [30] and synthetic polymers [21].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a newly emerging form of zero-dimensional carbon nanostructure, is constituted of few-layered graphene sheets with diameters below 100nm [31,32]. Its unique and superior properties have promised much potential use of this emergent nanolight for biosensing [19,33,34] and bioimaging [35,36] applications. These extraordinary properties include multicolour and tunable emission [37–40], broad excitation range [41], photochemical [42,43] and physicochemical stability [31,44], ease of functionalization [45], water solubility [46,47], low cytotoxicity [48,49] and excellent biocompatibility [50]. In addition to the intrinsic advantages of nanomaterials supporting their use as FRET acceptors, the broad excitation range coupled with tunable emission of GQDs allows the simple selection of an excitation wavelength that corresponds well with the absorption spectrum of an acceptor molecule while avoiding its direct excitation, thereby limiting the background interference of fluorescence sensing assays and therefore supporting the use of GQDs as FRET donors as well. Therefore, GQDs can function as either FRET acceptors or donors depending on the sensing platform desired. In addition, electron delocalization within the large sp2 network inherent to graphene-based nanostructures facilitates the physical adsorption of single-stranded DNA onto the nanomaterial surface, via non-covalent π–π stacking interactions and hydrophobic interactions with DNA nucleobases [51–53]. This therefore makes GQD a potential candidate for the trace detection of DNA. However, exploration of GQDs as potential FRET nanosensors in DNA detection, in particular as FRET nanoquenchers, still remains at a preliminary stage.

Two synthesis routes are available for GQD preparation: top-down [54–57] and bottom-up [58–60]. The top-down method – cutting and exfoliation of bulk graphene derivatives [61] – has been preferred thus far due to the limited availability of organic precursors and complex nature of the bottom-up synthesis route [62]. Recently, coal has been reported as an abundant and cost-effective raw material for the one-step top-down synthesis of GQDs [63]. Here, we explore the feasibility of GQDs synthesized from a related fossil fuel-derived product – calcined petroleum coke – as a cost-effective fluorescent detection platform for nucleic acid sensing. The simplicity and cost-effectiveness of our proposed DNA detection strategy alongside its ability to detect nucleic acid concentrations down to the picomolar range may eventually help to reduce the reliance on time-consuming and costly polymerase chain reaction (PCR) procedures in traditional DNA detection techniques [64], while not compromising its low detection limits. The results obtained in this paper will be beneficial towards the development of DNA sensors for various applications including paternity testing [65], ancestry determination [66] and early medical diagnosis.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a JEOL JEM-1010 instrument at an accelerating voltage of 80kV. Images were captured using SIS MegaView III digital camera (Soft Imaging Systems) and analyzed with AnalySIS v2.0 software. Samples were dried prior to measurements. HR-TEM was performed with an EFTEM Jeol 2200 FS microscope (Jeol, Japan). An acceleration voltage of 200keV was used for measurement. The sample was prepared by drop casting the suspension on a TEM grid (Cu; 200 mesh; Formvar/carbon) and drying at 60°C for 12h.

The particle size and zeta potential distributions were determined using Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS instrument Malvern Instruments, Malvern, England) in the dynamic light scattering (DLS) regime for particle size distribution. The zeta potential was measured by electrophoretic light scattering. DPSS laser (50mW, 532nm) was used as a light source and the signal was detected by avalanche photodiode. For the measurement of zeta potential, the sample was dispersed by ultrasonication (10min 15W−1) in 50mM phosphate buffer solution (diluted 1:1 by volume). The measurement was performed in polystyrene cuvettes with gold coated electrodes. The size distribution measurement was performed in PMMA cuvettes.

Wide-scan and high-resolution C1s X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were recorded using an ESCAProbeP spectrometer (Omicron Nanotechnology Ltd, Taunusstein, Germany) using monochromatic Al radiation (1486.7eV). Samples were prepared for XPS measurements by drop-casting the respective GQD solutions on gold coated silicon wafers, then allowed to dry at 50°C.

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum was recorded by a MCD D* detector using a NICOLET iS50R spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) affixed with a Smart SAGA Specular Reflectance accessory. Samples were prepared for XPS measurements by drop-casting the respective GQD solutions on gold coated silicon wafers, then allowed to dry at 50°C.
Raman spectroscopy spectra were obtained in backscattering geometry using an inVia Raman microscope (Renishaw, England), equipped with a CCD detector.

Fluorescence emission spectroscopy measurements were conducted using a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. λ ex of DNA sensing experiments involving GQDs was fixed at 490nm, and emission spectra were recorded over the range of 500–900nm. All measurements were conducted using a quartz glass cuvette, under room temperature conditions.

Concentrated sulfuric acid (60.0mL), nitric acid (22.0mL) and the calcined carbon precursor (300mg) were added into a reaction flask, then sonicated for 2h. The obtained reaction mixture was then heated at 100°C for 24h under stirring, then allowed to cool undisturbed to room temperature. The mixture was then poured into a beaker over ice (100g), and the pH was adjusted to 6.0 using sodium hydroxide solution (3M). The solution was then filtered with a nylon filter (0.45μm) and maintained at 0°C for 24h. Crystallized sodium sulfate was then removed using suction filtration, and the filtrate was dialysed for 5 days in distilled water until the conductivity dropped below 20μS. The dialysate was concentrated using a vacuum evaporator, affording the GQDs solution (100mL).

Comprehensive characterizations were first conducted to evaluate the structural and chemical properties of the GQDs obtained from coke derivative precursors. GQDs characterizations were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and Raman spectroscopy.

TEM was used to probe the structural morphology and crystallinity of the GQD particles [67,68]. Moderate to severe aggregation of GQDs were observed following sample drying for TEM imaging. Spherical dots were evident from the TEM images of GQDs (Fig. 1A). The hexagonal symmetry of the graphene crystal lattice can be observed using high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) in Fig. 1B and is further confirmed by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in the inset of Fig. 1A.

DLS was performed to obtain the size distribution profile of the GQDs in water (Fig. 2A) [69,70]. The hydrodynamic diameters of the GQDs demonstrate broad size distributions. DLS data revealed that the size of the GQD span from 30nm to 550nm, mainly concentrating at 55nm. The generally large lateral dimensions of the as-derived GQD particles may be attributed to the layered morphology of GQDs. The zeta potential of the GQDs was also measured for the analysis of particle surface charges (Fig. 2B) [71]. Zeta potential distribution was relatively broad showing population of quantum dots with differences in surface chemistry. The dominant peak at −40.9mV indicates highly hydrophilic water-stable GQDs; a small fraction of particles possess zeta potentials of −25mV and −15mV.

FT-IR was performed to ascertain the functional groups present in the GQDs (Fig. 4 ) [75]. The FT-IR spectrum revealed peaks at approximately 3432cm−1, 1724cm−1 and 1621cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of OH, CO and CC, respectively. The peak at 1437cm−1 may be attributed to the COH or CH bending modes, and the peak at 1068cm−1 corresponds to the CO stretching mode. FT-IR data further confirms the presence of hydrophilic carboxyl and hydroxyl functional groups in the GQDs.

Raman spectroscopy was performed to evaluate the density of defects in GQDs (Fig. 5 ) [76,77]. A D/G ratio of 0.44 was obtained, suggesting the generally low degree of structural defects present in GQDs synthesized by derivatives of coke. However, the shape and intensities of bands are also influenced by the excitation wavelength. The excitation was performed with UV laser (He-Cd laser, 325nm) where high cross-section of G-band led to its enhanced intensity in comparison with D-band [78].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Employing GQD as a fluorescence nanoquencher (FRET acceptor) in DNA analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 displays the schematic illustration of DNA detection based on GQD as the fluorescence nanoquencher. Detection of cDNA target is accomplished through the following procedure. For the first case, Part (A), FAM-Lprobe adsorbs onto the GQD upon undergoing incubation, via π–π stacking interactions and hydrophobic interactions. Hence, the fluorescence signal from FAM-Lprobe is quenched. In the second case, Part B, FAM-Lprobe is mixed with cDNA to allow hybridization of the two single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides, forming a double-stranded DNA assembly (ds–cDNA–FAM-Lprobe). Subsequently, the double-stranded DNA assembly is exposed to the GQD nanoquencher. Due to the lower affinity of the GQD towards dsDNA, the latter is not adsorbed onto the GQD surface and the fluorescence signal is retained.

The volume of the GQDs nanoquencher was first optimized, by monitoring changes in the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of FAM-Lprobe after incubating with increasing volumes of the nanoquencher (Fig. 6 ). An increase in the volume of the GQDs quencher resulted in an enhancement in quenching of the FAM-Lprobe fluorophore, which gradually tapered off and approached maximal quenching at approximately 100μL. Therefore, an optimum volume of 100μL was utilized for subsequent experiments.

Using the established optimum volume of the GQDs nanoquencher, the sensing performance of this nanoquencher was evaluated with increasing concentrations of cDNA target. A calibration graph based on the PL emission intensity plotted as a logarithmic function of cDNA concentration revealed a broad linear detection range stretching over 3 orders of magnitude, from 0.004nM to 4nM (Fig. 7 ). The increase in fluorescence intensity upon addition of more cDNA target is attributed to the formation of larger amounts of ds–cDNA–FAM-Lprobe hybrids, consequently resulting in a lower extent of adsorption onto the GQDs and poorer quenching efficiency, thus giving rise to a greater retention of PL emission.

In order to affirm the lack of interference by the cDNA target on the PL emission, a control study involving only the introduction of cDNA target to FAM-Lprobe was performed. Negligible influence on the PL intensity of FAM-Lprobe (brown line) was observed upon ds–cDNA–FAM-Lprobe hybrid formation (blue line) (Fig. 8 ). Herein, it can be concluded that any changes in fluorescence intensity may be solely attributed to the FAM-Lprobe.

Herein, we report for the first time, the development of a novel FRET fluorescence quenching platform based on coke-derived GQDs for DNA detection. GQDs obtained from calcined petroleum coke demonstrated the ability to detect DNA concentrations as low as 0.004nM, with a wide linear dynamic range of detection spanning across 3 orders of magnitude. The linear detection range was determined to be 0.004–4nM. The cost-effectiveness and large abundance of coke-derived precursors makes these GQDs a promising material for the development of affordable and efficient nanobiosensors for highly sensitive and selective nucleic acid detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: M.P. acknowledges a Tier 2 grant (MOE2013-T2-1-056; ARC 35/13) from the Ministry of Education, Singapore. Z.S. and K.K. were supported by Czech Science Foundation (GACR No. 15-09001S) and by specific university research (MSMT No. 20-SVV/2016). This work was supported by the project Advanced Functional Nanorobots (reg. No. CZ.02.1.01/0.0/0.0/15_003/0000444 financed by the EFRR).
